informal: Show that every linear map from a one-dimensional vector space to itself is multiplication by some scalar. More precisely, prove that if $\operatorname{dim} V=1$ and $T \in \mathcal{L}(V, V)$, then there exists $a \in \mathbf{F}$ such that $T v=a v$ for all $v \in V$.
lean:
theorem Axler_exercise_3_1 {F V : Type*}
  [AddCommGroup V] [Field F] [Module F V] [FiniteDimensional F V]
  (T : V →ₗ[F] V) (hT : finrank F V = 1) :
  ∃ c : F, ∀ v : V, T v = c • v
reasoning: The Lean signature takes an arbitrary field F and an F-module V, assumes finrank F V = 1, and concludes ∃ c : F, ∀ v, T v = c • v, which exactly matches the informal claim "if dim V = 1 then T is scalar multiplication". The [FiniteDimensional F V] hypothesis is redundant given finrank = 1 (a positive finrank already implies finite-dimensionality in Mathlib's convention, and even if it didn't, finite-dimensionality is implicit in the informal statement "dim V = 1"), so it does not strengthen or weaken the claim. AddCommGroup + Module F + Field F is the standard Mathlib encoding of an F-vector space. The quantifier structure (∃ scalar, ∀ vector) matches exactly.
